ian_adrian_ahmed.name: Ian Adrian Ahmed
ian_adrian_ahmed.summary:
Out of North Carolina, Ian Adrian Ahmed is in the early chapters of a competitive journey, currently competing at the U15 level, with 3 entries across 1 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

Ian is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
ian_adrian_ahmed.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ian Adrian Ahmed, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 3 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Ian is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19), and so far, the only thing Ian has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Ian saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ian plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 3 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ian has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ian showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in BS U15, which means Ian traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19), where Ian entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Ian paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Ian is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Ian wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Ian, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

ian_darmawan.name: Ian Darmawan
ian_darmawan.summary:
A Texas competitor, Ian Darmawan has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Ian's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

A true triple-threat, Ian competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (1 entry) • ORC top-3 finisher.

Ian has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 8 different players across the career:
  • Maya Lekkala — a trusted XD partner (4 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Ian has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Ian isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Ian brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
ian_darmawan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ian Darmawan, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-49. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Ian has 24 career entries across 10 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Ian saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U11-U13) with 2 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ian plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 24 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Ian has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Ian's texts. The go-to partner is Arjun Aditya Hariharan (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U13 — we hope Arjun Aditya Hariharan has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Ian has shown up 1 time with a best of top-49. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Ian has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ian has 16 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ian showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U15, which means Ian traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Ian entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Ian paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 24 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Ian. Ian just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Ian has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Ian. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Ian: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

ian_yip.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ian Yip, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Ian has 18 career entries across 6 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 6% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Ian saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2022 NE CRC in XD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Ian's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 12 entries — a stretch where Ian actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Ian is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ian plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Ian is 0-for-6. Zero titles in 6 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Ian and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Ian needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Ian has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Ian's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U17 — we hope Napat Srisalaipaisal has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Ian's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Ian has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Ian has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Ian has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ian has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ian showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U17, which means Ian traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Ian entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Ian paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 18 entries, and 17 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ian looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ian's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ian has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ian. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

ianson_chan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ianson Chan, from Maryland, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 8 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Ianson is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 Frisco South ORC, and so far, the only thing Ianson has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Ianson saga began at 2024 Frisco South ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ianson plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 8 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Ianson entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Ianson, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Ianson. We will be here, taking notes.

A North Carolina competitor, Imon Sahu broke through with a tournament title, currently competing at the U13 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 15 entries across 7 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

What makes Imon especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 7 different doubles partners over the years, Imon adapts to anyone:
  • Tapaswin Gandhavalla (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in BD

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 1 title across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Imon is still ascending.

Imon has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Imon's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
imon_sahu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Imon Sahu, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 1 win across 15 entries in 7 tournaments. That is a 7% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Imon shows up to a tournament, Imon pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Imon saga began at 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19) with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2025 Fortius South CRC in BD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Imon's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Imon plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Imon is 0-for-7. Zero titles in 7 tries. The best singles result is a 9th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Imon and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Imon needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Imon has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19) in BD U11 — we hope Mehan Murthysundar has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Imon's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Imon has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Imon has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Imon showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U13, which means Imon traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 Fortius South CRC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC, where Imon entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 37th, which is a polite way of saying Imon paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 15 entries, and 14 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Imon looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Imon's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Imon has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Imon. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

irene_xu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Irene Xu, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 62 career entries in 24 tournaments, Irene has 1 title — a 2% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 61 entries where Irene drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 62 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Irene saga began at 2022 NorCal ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC in GD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Irene's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 32 entries — a stretch where Irene actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Irene is 0-for-10. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Irene plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Irene is 0-for-24. Zero titles in 24 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Irene and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Irene needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Irene has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Irene's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Edwin Huang: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Edwin Huang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Edwin Huang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Irene's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Irene entered 2 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Irene's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Irene and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Irene has shown up 11 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Irene has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Irene is not just losing locally — Irene is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Xu family travels cross-country so Irene can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Irene has 33 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Irene showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15, which means Irene traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Irene entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Irene paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 62 entries, and 61 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Irene looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Irene's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Irene has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Irene. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

  • Aakash Unnikrishnan (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Isaac brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
isaac_joseph.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Isaac Joseph, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Isaac has 44 career entries across 16 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Isaac saga began at 2022 South Houston CRC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Isaac plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 44 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 18 of them, to be precise. Isaac has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Isaac's texts. The go-to partner is Isha Haldankar (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Isha Haldankar has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Isaac has shown up 2 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Isaac is not just losing locally — Isaac is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Joseph family travels cross-country so Isaac can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Isaac has 27 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Isaac showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15, which means Isaac traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Isaac entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Isaac paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 44 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Isaac. Isaac just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Isaac has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Isaac. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Isaac: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

isaac_yang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Isaac Yang, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-2. Top-2 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 34 career entries in 12 tournaments, Isaac has 7 titles — a 21% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 3 entries where Isaac drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 34 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Isaac saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Isaac's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 5 titles in 11 entries — a stretch where Isaac actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Isaac is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Isaac experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Isaac has reached 9 championship matches across this career. That is 9 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 9 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19), where Isaac entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BD U17, BS U19). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Isaac does own 7 actual titles, so it is not like Isaac has never won anything. But 9 finals losses versus 7 wins means Isaac is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Isaac plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 3 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Isaac can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Isaac has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Isaac's texts. The go-to partner is Weslie Chen (5 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Isaac entered 9 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-9. The Selection committee looked at Isaac's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Isaac and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Isaac 3 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Then there is the seeding situation. Isaac has been seeded in 6 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Isaac takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2023 U.S. Selection Event in U19 BS, and finishing 4th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Isaac. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Isaac has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Isaac is not just losing locally — Isaac is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 21% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yang family travels cross-country so Isaac can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 7 titles to the name, 34 entries, and 27 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Isaac looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Isaac's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Isaac has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Isaac. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Isabelle is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, and so far, the only thing Isabelle has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Isabelle saga began at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Isabelle has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Isabelle showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U19, which means Isabelle traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Isabelle is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Isabelle wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Isabelle, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

isaiah_melwyn_alleluia.name: Isaiah Melwyn Alleluia
isaiah_melwyn_alleluia.summary:
A Texas competitor, Isaiah Melwyn Alleluia has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U19 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 23 entries in 12 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Notably, Isaiah has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Isaiah especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Isaiah has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Isaiah has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Isaiah brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
isaiah_melwyn_alleluia.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Isaiah Melwyn Alleluia, from Texas, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-33. Top-33 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 23 career entries in 12 tournaments, Isaiah has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 22 entries where Isaiah drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 23 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Isaiah saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U11-U13) with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Isaiah plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 23 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Isaiah has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Isaiah's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Eshani Pahuja has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Isaiah entered 2 events with results ranging from top-33 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Isaiah's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Isaiah and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Isaiah has shown up 2 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Isaiah has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Isaiah is not just losing locally — Isaiah is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Alleluia family travels cross-country so Isaiah can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Isaiah has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Isaiah showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Isaiah traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Isaiah entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Isaiah paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 23 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Isaiah. Isaiah just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Isaiah has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Isaiah. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Isaiah: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

ish_modi.name: Ish Modi

Notably, Ish has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Ish is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries) • 1 ORC title.

With 6 different doubles partners over the years, Ish adapts to anyone:
  • Anirud Veldanda (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in BD

The 2021-2022 season tells a story of acceleration, with 1 title across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Ish is still ascending.

Ish has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning NE, SoCal, South, Ish isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Ish's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
ish_modi.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ish Modi, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 12 career entries in 5 tournaments, Ish has 1 title — a 8% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 11 entries where Ish drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 12 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Ish saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 7th. The first title would not come until 2021 NE ORC (U11-U13) in BD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Ish's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ish plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Ish is 0-for-4. Zero titles in 4 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Ish and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Ish needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Ish has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U13 — we hope Nithesh Haribabu has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Ish entered 2 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Ish's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Ish and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Ish has shown up 2 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Ish has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Ish has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ish has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ish showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U15, which means Ish traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Ish entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Ish paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 12 entries, and 11 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ish looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ish's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ish has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ish. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

ishaan_a_pillutla.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ishaan A Pillutla, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 27 career entries in 11 tournaments, Ishaan has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 26 entries where Ishaan drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 27 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Ishaan saga began at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ishaan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 27 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Ishaan has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Vihaan Vinod: 9 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 9 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Vihaan Vinod has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BD U15 — we hope Vihaan Vinod has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Ishaan entered 2 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Ishaan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Ishaan and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Ishaan has shown up 3 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Ishaan is not just losing locally — Ishaan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Pillutla family travels cross-country so Ishaan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ishaan has 23 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ishaan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Ishaan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Ishaan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Ishaan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Ishaan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Ishaan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Ishaan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Ishaan. We will be here, taking notes.

ishaan_keshri.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ishaan Keshri, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-97. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Ishaan has 25 career entries across 12 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 4% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Ishaan saga began at 2023 South CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE OLC (NVBC) in XD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Ishaan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 12 entries — a stretch where Ishaan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Ishaan is 0-for-4. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ishaan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Ishaan is 0-for-12. Zero titles in 12 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Ishaan and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Ishaan needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Ishaan has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Ishan Kanchi: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Ishan Kanchi has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17 — we hope Vanessa Njam has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Ishaan's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Ishaan has shown up 1 time with a best of top-97. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Ishaan has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ishaan has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ishaan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 97th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15, which means Ishaan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC, where Ishaan entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Ishaan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 25 entries, and 24 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ishaan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ishaan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ishaan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ishaan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

The Ishan saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U11-U13) with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ishan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 28 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Ishan has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Shrihan Reddy Pingili: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Shrihan Reddy Pingili has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BD U15 — we hope Karthik Selvakumar has emotionally recovered.

Ishan has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Ishan has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ishan has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ishan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15, which means Ishan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Ishan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Ishan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 28 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Ishan. Ishan just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Ishan has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Ishan. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Ishan: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

ishi_reddy.name: Ishi Reddy
ishi_reddy.summary:
Representing Illinois, Ishi Reddy has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-3 in GD U15, GS U15. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Ishi belongs with the best in the country.

Notably, Ishi has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Ishi is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 7 singles titles, 5 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (14 entries) • Junior Nationals top-3 (GD U15, GS U15) • 3 ORC titles.

Diving into the match data reveals: Ishi boasts a solid 69% win rate over 71 matches, 33 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a positive +428 point differential across the career.

Ishi has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 22 different players across the career:
  • Clarinda Jin (current primary partner) — 9 events together this season alone with 3 titles, building serious momentum (25 total events in GD/U15/U17, spanning 4 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 7 podium finishes across 4 tournaments. Ishi is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Ishi has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Ishi has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Ishi has been seeded #1 or #2 in 12 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 5 seasons and 14 titles, Ishi has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
ishi_reddy.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ishi Reddy, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 104 career entries in 36 tournaments, Ishi has 14 titles — a 13% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 6 entries where Ishi drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 104 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Ishi saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 3 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2022 Midwest CRC in GS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Ishi's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 5 titles in 24 entries — a stretch where Ishi actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 11 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Ishi experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Ishi has reached 9 championship matches across this career. That is 9 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 9 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2021 Egret Midwest OLC, where Ishi entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U13, GD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (7 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Ishi does own 14 actual titles, so it is not like Ishi has never won anything. But 9 finals losses versus 14 wins means Ishi is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ishi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 7 singles, 5 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Ishi can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Ishi has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Ishi's texts. The most successful partnership is with Clarinda Jin — 25 events, 5 titles — and honestly, Clarinda Jin deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Clarinda Jin a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Anirud Veldanda has emotionally recovered.

Of Ishi's 14 career titles, 11 came at local events versus 3 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Ishi feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Ishi entered 14 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Ishi's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Ishi and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Ishi 9 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Then there is the seeding situation. Ishi has been seeded in 20 events and underperformed the seeding 5 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Ishi takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U17, and finishing 6th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Ishi. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Ishi has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Ishi is not just losing locally — Ishi is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 13% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Reddy family travels cross-country so Ishi can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ishi has 22 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ishi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in XD U15, which means Ishi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Ishi entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Ishi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 14 titles to the name, 104 entries, and 90 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ishi looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ishi's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ishi has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ishi. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.
