ivey_x_bao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ivey X. Bao, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. This is a player who has stood at the top of the podium at Junior Nationals — the crown jewel of the American junior badminton calendar — and won GD U11 - Position 5-8. That is a real, verified, indisputable accomplishment, and no one can ever take it away. But here's what they CAN take away: any illusion that the rest of this resume is equally impressive. Because we are looking at 12 career entries across 4 tournaments, 1 total wins, and 11 losses. A 8% win rate that suggests the Nationals title was less a coronation and more a cosmic accident that Ivey has been unsuccessfully trying to replicate ever since. The story started at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, and it has been a wild, inconsistent, deeply roastable ride from there.

The Ivey saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Ivey burst onto the scene with a title in GD U11 - Position 5-8 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Ivey has delivered on some of those promises: 1 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Ivey promised and what Ivey has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2020-2021 season — 1 title in 3 entries — a stretch where Ivey actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Ivey is 0-for-9. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ivey plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Ivey is 0-for-4. Zero titles in 4 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Ivey and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Ivey needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Ivey has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Renee Liu (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2022 South Frisco ORC in XD U13 — we hope Ariston Yu Feng Goh has emotionally recovered.

Ivey has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Ivey has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ivey has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ivey showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2022 South Frisco ORC in GS U13, which means Ivey traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 South Frisco ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 12 entries, and 11 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ivey looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ivey's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ivey has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ivey. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

ivy_chang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ivy Chang, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Ivy has 36 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 7 of them — a 19% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Ivy saga began at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2024 Midwest CRC in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Ivy's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 4 titles in 11 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Ivy experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Ivy has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2026 CBA Midwest OLC, where Ivy entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U15, XD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (1 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Ivy does own 7 actual titles, so it is not like Ivy has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 7 wins means Ivy is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ivy plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 4 singles, 0 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Ivy can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Ivy has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Ivy's texts. The go-to partner is Adrian Zhang (5 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Allen Wang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Ivy's 7 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Ivy has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9. Tournament committees have handed Ivy a #1 or #2 seed 5 times, and Ivy has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2023 Egret Midwest OLC in GS U11, where Ivy was seeded #2 and finished 4th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Ivy has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Ivy has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ivy has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ivy showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13, which means Ivy traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Ivy entered 3 events with results ranging from 25th to 49th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Ivy paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 7 titles to the name, 36 entries, and 29 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ivy looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ivy's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ivy has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ivy. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 6 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.
